1: YHWH's response to Moses's theodicy complaint (Exodus 5:22-23): “Now you will see what I will do to Pharaoh: Because of my mighty hand he will let them go; because of my mighty hand he will drive them out of his land.” The phrase beyad chazaqah (“by a strong/mighty hand”) becomes the defining description of the Exodus throughout Deuteronomy (Exodus 4:34; Exodus 5:15; Exodus 6:21; Exodus 7:8, 19; Exodus 9:26; Exodus 26:8; Exodus 34:12). Paradoxically, it is Pharaoh's hard heart that will result in his driving Israel out — he will force them to leave not because he wants to, but because the divine pressure will leave him no alternative.
2: “God also said to Moses, ‘I am YHWH.’” The self-declaration ani YHWH (I am YHWH) is the covenant formula that opens and closes the seven-fold promise of Exodus 6:2-8. It brackets the entire speech: YHWH declares his name in v2 and reaffirms it in v8. The name YHWH is both the basis and the guarantee of the promises — they stand because the one who makes them is the I AM, whose word is his very existence. Paul structures the covenant promise similarly: “I am the Lord your God” — the identity claim precedes and grounds the imperatives.
3: “I appeared to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob as God Almighty [El Shaddai], but by my name YHWH I did not make myself fully known to them.” The interpretive crux of the divine name: the patriarchs knew and used the name YHWH (Genesis 15:7; Exodus 28:13; Exodus 32:9), yet here YHWH says he was not known by that name. The resolution most consistent with the text: noda'ti (make known) refers not to nominal knowledge but to experiential revelation. The Exodus will be the defining act that fills the name YHWH with redemptive content — the plagues, the sea-crossing, the covenant at Sinai are the revelation of what YHWH means. The patriarchs knew the name; the Exodus generation will know YHWH through his acts.
4: “I also established my covenant with them to give them the land of Canaan, where they resided as foreigners.” The patriarchs were gerim (sojourners, resident aliens) in Canaan — they never owned it except for burial ground (Gen 23). The covenant promise was for a land they only glimpsed. YHWH's remembering the covenant (v5) initiates the action that will fulfill what was promised in Abraham's lifetime. The land promise grounds the Exodus in the long arc of covenant history; the liberation from Egypt is not a new story but the continuation of Genesis.
5: “Moreover, I have heard the groaning of the Israelites, whom the Egyptians are enslaving, and I have remembered my covenant.” Zakar (remember) in Hebrew covenantal discourse is not merely mental recall but active covenant engagement: when YHWH “remembers,” he acts. The same verb describes God remembering Noah before the flood receded (Genesis 8:1) and remembering Rachel when he opened her womb (Genesis 30:22). Divine memory is performative. The groaning (ne'aqah) echoes Exodus 2:24 — YHWH heard their cry and remembered his covenant. The word connects this speech directly to the Exodus narrative's opening movement.
6: The first of seven “I will” promises: “I will bring you out from under the yoke of the Egyptians.” The verb yatza (bring out/go out) is the Exodus's defining verb — it gives the book its name in later tradition (sefer yetziat mitzraim, the book of the going out from Egypt). The second promise adds the liberation language: “I will deliver you from their slavery” (netziticha). The third — “I will redeem you with an outstretched arm” (ga'alti) — uses the technical term ga'al (kinsman-redeemer), the go'el obligation: YHWH acts as Israel's next of kin to redeem those sold into slavery (Leviticus 25:47-55). YHWH is Israel's divine go'el.
7: “I will take you as my own people, and I will be your God.” Laqachti (I will take) and hayiti (I will be) — the fourth and fifth promises form the covenant formula: you will be my people and I will be your God. This bilateral formula (Deuteronomy 26:17-18; Jeremiah 7:23; Exodus 11:4; Ezekiel 36:28; Zechariah 8:8) is the heart of the Sinai covenant, restated throughout the prophets as the thing lost in exile and restored in the new covenant (Jeremiah 31:33; Ezekiel 37:27). The formula appears here before Sinai, establishing that the covenant relationship precedes and grounds the legal code: YHWH takes Israel before he gives the Torah.
8: The sixth and seventh promises: “I will bring you to the land I swore with uplifted hand to give to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob; I will give it to you as a possession.” Heivaiti (I will bring in) and natati (I will give). The speech ends as it began: ani YHWH (I am YHWH). The seven-fold I-will promise is structured as a covenant renewal speech that anticipates the Sinai covenant's formal structure. The uplifted hand (nasati et-yadi) is the oath gesture of ANE treaty-making — YHWH swore the land promise to the patriarchs by raising his hand, the most solemn form of divine commitment.
9: “Moses reported this to the Israelites, but they did not listen to him because of their discouragement and harsh labor.” Miqotzer ruach umeavoda kasha — “from shortness of spirit and from harsh labor.” Kotzer ruach (shortness of breath/spirit) is the Hebrew idiom for anguish that leaves no room for hope. The people's covenant deafness here is caused by oppression, not by moral failure or unbelief. This distinction matters pastorally: the inability to receive a hopeful word under crushing circumstances is not the same as willful rejection of YHWH. The Exodus narrative shows compassion for the psychologically crushed. YHWH does not rebuke Israel for this deafness; he simply continues to act on their behalf.
10: The brief transition verse prepares the second commission to speak to Pharaoh. The repetition of the commission after the failed first attempt (Exodus 5) is not redundancy but escalation: YHWH is not deterred by Pharaoh's refusal or Israel's deafness. The narrative loop — commission, refusal, complaint, renewal — is the structural pattern of prophetic literature. Ezekiel will receive a similar “whether they hear or refuse to hear” commission (Ezekiel 2:5, 7; Exodus 3:11).
11: “Go in, tell Pharaoh king of Egypt to let the Israelites go out of his land.” The renewed commission is identical in content to the original (Exodus 5:1). The divine purpose is not changed by failure, by hardened hearts, or by exhausted audiences. The repetition of the commission emphasizes its unconditional character: YHWH's word goes forward regardless of reception. This is the characteristic of genuine prophetic commission: Isaiah's commission explicitly anticipated rejection (Isaiah 6:9-10); Jeremiah's word was tested by consistent rejection.
12: Moses's renewed objection: “If the Israelites will not listen to me, why would Pharaoh listen to me, since I speak with faltering lips?” Aral sefatayim — “uncircumcised lips.” The metaphor of uncircumcision as spiritual unfitness or impurity is used for lips (here), hearts (Leviticus 26:41; Jeremiah 9:26), and ears (Jeremiah 6:10). The circumcision rite removes the covering that blocks full participation in the covenant. Moses's “uncircumcised lips” may be his idiom for his perceived inability to speak persuasively — or it may carry a deeper sense of unworthiness to mediate the divine word. YHWH's response is to commission both Moses and Aaron together.
13: YHWH commands both Moses and Aaron regarding “the Israelites and Pharaoh king of Egypt, to bring the Israelites out of Egypt.” The double commission — to Israel and to Pharaoh simultaneously — shows the two-front nature of the mission: Moses and Aaron must address both the oppressed community (which doesn't listen, v9) and the oppressive ruler (who won't hear). The joint commission of Moses and Aaron legitimizes Aaron's co-leadership, not merely as Moses's spokesman but as a named bearer of the divine mandate.
14: The genealogical insert (Exodus 6:14-25) interrupts the narrative to establish the credentials of Moses and Aaron by Levitical lineage. The genealogy opens with Reuben and Simeon (the two older brothers), then focuses exclusively on Levi — Reuben and Simeon are listed to establish the ordinal position (third son = Levi) before tracing the line that matters. This selectivity is characteristic of biblical genealogies: they are not census records but legitimating structures that identify who carries the covenant line.
15: Simeon's sons include Shaul “the son of a Canaanite woman” — an unusual detail that acknowledges ethnic mixing in the patriarchal generation. The genealogy does not present a pure ethnic lineage but an honest record that includes intermarriage with Canaanites even within the founding families. This is consistent with the Pentateuch's general candor about Israel's mixed origins.
16: Levi's sons: Gershon, Kohath, Merari — the three Levitical clans that will organize the transport and service of the tabernacle (Num 3-4). Their roles in the wilderness are already implicit in the genealogy. Levi lived 137 years — the only patriarch whose lifespan is given in this genealogy, emphasizing his foundational importance for the priestly line.
17: The sons of Gershon: Libni and Shimei. The Gershonites were responsible for the curtains, coverings, and hangings of the tabernacle (Numbers 3:25-26). Their sub-clan names are given to maintain the completeness of the Levitical register that will govern tabernacle worship.
18: Kohath's sons: Amram, Izhar, Hebron, Uzziel. Kohath lived 133 years. The Kohathites were the most honored Levitical clan, responsible for carrying the ark and the most sacred furnishings of the tabernacle (Numbers 3:29-32; Exodus 4:4-15). Their care of the ark was so strict that even touching the furnishings while carrying them was forbidden (Numbers 4:15). Amram, Moses's father, heads the Kohathite line.
19: Merari's sons: Mahli and Mushi. The Merarites carried the tabernacle's structural frames, bars, pillars, and bases (Numbers 3:36-37; Exodus 4:31-32). The three-clan structure (Gershon/Kohath/Merari) organized the entire wilderness transportation and assembly of YHWH's dwelling — it is already implicit in the genealogy that these clans will serve the tabernacle before the tabernacle is built.
20: Amram married Jochebed, his father's sister — a union later prohibited in Leviticus 18:12. The pre-Sinai era is governed by different rules; the Mosaic law formally prohibits this marriage, but it is recorded without moral comment in the genealogy. Moses and Aaron are born of this union. Amram's 137-year lifespan places Moses's birth in the context of extraordinary longevity still echoing the patriarchal period.
21: Izhar's sons: Korah, Nepheg, Zichri. Korah will lead the rebellion against Moses in Numbers 16 — his name is here introduced quietly in the genealogy, decades before his challenge to Mosaic authority. The genealogy thus plants the seed of the later conflict. Korah's rebellion (Numbers 16:1-35) will claim the holiness of the whole congregation against Moses's exclusive mediation, and be answered by divine fire.
22: Uzziel's sons: Mishael, Elzaphan, Sithri. Elzaphan (also Elizaphan) will appear in Leviticus 10:4 when Moses instructs him and Mishael to carry out the bodies of Nadab and Abihu after they die before YHWH for offering unauthorized fire. The genealogy quietly introduces figures who will play roles in the later narrative, creating a sense of providential preparation.
23: Aaron married Elisheba daughter of Amminadab and sister of Nahshon, who bore him Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar. Amminadab is the prince of Judah (Numbers 1:7) — Aaron's marriage to the Judahite princess connects the priestly line (Levi) to the royal line (Judah) in the founding generation. Nahshon was the military leader of Judah in the wilderness. The four sons: Nadab and Abihu die for unauthorized worship (Lev 10); Eleazar and Ithamar continue the Aaronic priesthood. Eleazar is the father of Phinehas (v25), who will receive the eternal priesthood covenant (Numbers 25:13).
24: Korah's sons: Assir, Elkanah, Abiasaph — the Korahite clans. Despite their father's rebellion (Num 16), the sons of Korah did not die with him (Numbers 26:11) and became temple singers. The Psalms of the Sons of Korah (Ps 42-49, 84-85, 87-88) bear their name. The genealogy thus contains the seed of both rebellion (Korah) and worship (the Korah psalms) — the same lineage carries both the worst and the most beautiful responses to YHWH's presence.
25: Eleazar son of Aaron married a daughter of Putiel, who bore him Phinehas. The name Putiel (possibly Egyptian in origin — Puti-el, “given by God”) may suggest Egyptian ancestry, consistent with the intermarriage that characterizes this generation. Phinehas will receive the covenant of a perpetual priesthood (Numbers 25:13) for his zealous action at Baal-Peor. The genealogy ends with these “heads of the Levite clans” — emphasizing that the genealogy's purpose was legitimation, not census.
26: “It was this same Aaron and Moses to whom YHWH said, ‘Bring the Israelites out of Egypt by their divisions.’” The resumptive summary after the genealogy identifies the two by name (reversing the usual Moses-then-Aaron order to Aaron-then-Moses, then restating as Moses-and-Aaron in v27) — a deliberate stylistic device that emphasizes their joint authority. The genealogy has established who they are; this verse confirms that they are the YHWH-commissioned leaders.
27: “They were the ones who spoke to Pharaoh king of Egypt about bringing the Israelites out — this same Moses and Aaron.” The repetition is the literary device called Wiederaufnahme (resumptive repetition): the narrative thread is explicitly picked up after the genealogical interruption. The doubled identification (v26 and v27) signals the end of the insert and the return to the main narrative. This device appears throughout the OT when material is inserted into a narrative flow (cf. Genesis 2:4-5 after Exodus 1:1-2:3; 1 Kings 22:46 after the Jehoshaphat narrative).
28: “On the day YHWH spoke to Moses in Egypt” — the resumptive narrative reconnects with the commissioning dialogue that was interrupted by the genealogy. The phrase is not a temporal specification (this was not a single day in the usual sense) but a narrative frame that says: “returning to that conversation.” The Egyptian setting is emphasized — the commission takes place on Egyptian soil, in the heart of the oppressor's domain, not yet at the mountain of God. YHWH's word is effective wherever it is spoken.
29: “He said to him, ‘I am YHWH. Tell Pharaoh king of Egypt everything I tell you.’” The commission is restated verbatim: ani YHWH (I am YHWH) grounds the command; kol asher ani dover eleicha (“everything I tell you”) demands complete transmission without selection or editing. The prophetic obligation is total fidelity to the received word. Jeremiah 26:2 will express the same obligation: “speak all the words I command you; do not omit a word.”
30: Moses's final objection, restating v12: “Since I speak with faltering lips, why would Pharaoh listen to me?” The narrative ends this chapter where it began — with Moses's expressed inadequacy. The repetition (Exodus 6:12 and 30) forms an inclusio around the genealogy, framing the Levitical insert within Moses's ongoing reluctance. Chapter 7 will break through the objection with YHWH's direct answer: “I have made you like God to Pharaoh, and Aaron your brother will be your prophet.” The genealogy's insertion between the two objections may be intentional: Moses's lineage grounds his authority even as he disclaims it.
